metadata.notes:
agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: n/a

assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: partial
daemon_capability: incapable; driver_capability: n/a

## Scenario

user-blocking-plan-mode-approval: a session is started in plan mode and prompted to make a plan; the agent proposes a plan and then PAUSES, sitting in a daemon-observable `waiting` state, until the user approves (or rejects) the plan before any work proceeds. The user-observable signal (scenario-meanings, anchored on the claudecode cell) is a state_transition→waiting that PERSISTS — the session does not return to ready until the user acts on the plan. This is the same waiting path as user-blocking-question, reached via the plan-approval branch rather than a structured question. The cell asserts entry into that waiting episode.

## Agent supports it (agent_supports: partial)

opencode ships a built-in `plan` agent/mode (selectable in the TUI and via the `--agent plan` / agent config). BUT opencode's plan mode is a TOOL-RESTRICTION mode, not an approval gate: the plan agent runs with edit/write/bash tools disabled (read-only), so the model is constrained to read and reason without mutating the workspace. It does NOT emit an ExitPlanMode-style proposal that PAUSES the turn waiting for the user to approve before continuing. There is no blocking plan-review gate: the plan turn runs to completion (ready→working→ready) like any other turn; the only difference from a normal turn is which tools were available, not a session that sits paused awaiting approval. So opencode has a plan MODE (partial — the capability is named and present) but not the plan-APPROVAL primitive this scenario asserts (a waiting→approve transition). agent_supports is partial, not yes, for exactly this reason; and not no, because a plan mode does exist.

## Why the daemon cannot observe it (daemon_capability: incapable)

Two independent reasons, the second decisive:

1. **No blocking approval gate exists to observe.** Because opencode plan mode is tool-restriction, not an approval pause, there is no waiting episode for ANY observer to see — the plan turn completes and the session settles to ready. There is no waiting→approve transition in the session lifecycle at all.

2. **The opencode adapter has no waiting path.** Even if opencode emitted a plan-approval pause, the daemon could not surface it. opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter (core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/agent.go): the daemon reads session state straight from the SQLite store via the parser/watcher (parser.go, watcher.go). That code has NO waiting / user-blocking / ExitPlanMode / plan-approval branch whatsoever — grep across core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/*.go for waiting|StateWaiting|user-block|exitplan|approv returns nothing but an unrelated comment. The watcher's terminal-turn decision (watcher.go isTerminalPart / scanParts) recognizes only step-finish parts; the store's part schema records step-start/step-finish/text/tool parts, none of which carry a plan-approval / blocking-finish signal. So the daemon has no store signal it could classify as `waiting` for a plan gate, and no code path that would emit one. daemon_capability is incapable.

This mirrors the sibling opencode/user-blocking-question cell (agent_supports:no, daemon_capability:n/a — opencode has no structured blocking-question primitive that surfaces a daemon-observable waiting state from the store). user-blocking-plan-mode-approval is the plan-approval analog: opencode HAS a plan mode (so agent_supports is partial rather than no), but it is the wrong KIND of plan mode (tool-restriction, not approval-gate), so it produces no waiting state, and the store/adapter has no waiting branch to observe one anyway.

## Routing (per implement preconditions)

daemon_capability == incapable → STOP, applicable_false. There is no daemon-observable behavior to record; recording a headless plan-mode turn would capture an ordinary ready→working→ready arc and prove the WRONG thing (it would assert a waiting state that does not and cannot arise). No recipe, no recording, no expected.jsonl. by_adapter.opencode is set applicable:false with a scope_note; the cell is frozen at the assessment stage.

## What this is NOT

- NOT a driver_gap: this is not a case where opencode would emit a waiting state if only the driver could deliver some primitive (contrast user-esc-interrupt gap:interrupt or tool-gate-permission-prompt gap:permission-approval, where the agent CAN block and the daemon parses the signal, but the headless driver can't elicit it). Here there is no blocking plan-approval primitive in the agent at all, and no waiting branch in the adapter to surface it — so it is a daemon/agent INCAPABILITY, not a tooling gap. driver_capability is n/a.
- NOT agent_supports:no: opencode genuinely ships a plan agent/mode, so the capability is partially present — just the tool-restriction flavor, not the approval-gate flavor this scenario asserts.

## What would unblock it

Nothing in scope. It would require BOTH (a) opencode growing a real plan-APPROVAL gate that pauses a turn awaiting user approval (an upstream agent change — its current plan mode is tool-restriction by design), AND (b) the opencode adapter growing a store signal + parser/watcher branch that classifies such a pause as `waiting`. Until both exist there is nothing daemon-observable to record.

## Confidence

0.85: the decisive facts are read directly from source — the opencode adapter has no waiting/plan-approval branch (core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/parser.go + watcher.go; verified by grep), and opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter reading state from SQLite parts that carry no plan-approval signal. The agent-side fact (opencode plan mode is a read-only tool-restriction mode, not an approval gate) matches opencode's documented built-in plan agent and the dispatcher's verified reassessment. Not higher because the agent-side claim rests on opencode's documented plan-agent semantics rather than a fresh live plan-turn probe — but a live probe could only confirm the absence of a waiting state, which the adapter is structurally unable to emit regardless.
caveats: opencode plan mode is a TOOL-RESTRICTION (read-only: edit/write/bash disabled) mode, NOT a blocking plan-approval gate. The plan turn runs to completion (ready→working→ready) with no pause for user approval, so there is no waiting→approve transition for any observer to see. agent_supports is partial (a plan mode exists) but the approval-gate primitive this scenario asserts does not.

daemon_capability:incapable — the opencode adapter (lone ProcessOwnedStore) has NO waiting / user-blocking / ExitPlanMode / plan-approval branch in parser.go or watcher.go; grep across core/adapters/inbound/agents/opencode/*.go for waiting|StateWaiting|user-block|exitplan|approv returns nothing relevant. Even if opencode emitted a plan pause, the daemon has no store signal or code path to classify it as waiting.

NOT a driver_gap: there is no agent primitive to elicit and no adapter branch to surface it, so no live-REPL driver would change the verdict (contrast the opencode user-esc-interrupt / tool-gate-permission-prompt gap cells, where the agent CAN block and the daemon could parse it). driver_capability is n/a.

Sibling cell: opencode/user-blocking-question is agent_supports:no / daemon_capability:n/a (no structured blocking-question primitive observable from the store). This plan-approval analog is the same waiting-unobservability, with agent_supports:partial because a (tool-restriction) plan mode does exist.

sources.note: no waiting / user-blocking / ExitPlanMode / plan-approval branch — parses step-start/step-finish/text/tool parts and Task/todo snapshots only; no signal classifiable as a plan-approval waiting state
isTerminalPart / scanParts (lines ~423-499) recognize only step-finish parts as terminal; no waiting branch, so the daemon cannot emit a waiting state for a plan-approval gate
opencode is the lone ProcessOwnedStore adapter — daemon reads session state from the SQLite store; the store part schema carries no plan-approval / blocking-finish signal to classify as waiting

sibling waiting cell: opencode has no structured blocking-question primitive observable from the store (agent_supports:no, daemon_capability:n/a). This plan-approval analog shares the waiting-unobservability; agent_supports:partial here because a tool-restriction plan mode exists

claudecode anchor: user-blocking-plan-mode-approval asserts a state_transition→waiting via the ExitPlanMode branch (a blocking plan-review gate). opencode's plan mode is tool-restriction, not an ExitPlanMode-style approval gate, so this branch has no opencode analog

recipe.notes: scope_note: opencode plan mode is a TOOL-RESTRICTION (read-only) mode, not a blocking plan-approval gate — the plan turn runs to completion (ready→working→ready) with no pause for user approval, so there is no daemon-observable waiting→approval transition. agent_supports:partial (a plan mode exists, but the wrong KIND — tool-restriction, not approval-gate), daemon_capability:incapable. Even if opencode paused for approval, the opencode adapter (lone ProcessOwnedStore) has NO waiting/user-blocking/ExitPlanMode branch in parser.go/watcher.go to classify it as waiting (grep returns nothing relevant). NOT a driver_gap (no agent primitive to elicit, no adapter branch to surface). Mirrors the sibling opencode/user-blocking-question cell (no daemon-observable structured-blocking waiting state from the SQLite store). No recipe, no recording; frozen at assessment. See replaydata/agents/opencode/scenarios/user-blocking-plan-mode-approval/assessment.json.
